content.text: The Mental Model Shift

Learning React is not just learning a new API — it is adopting a different way of thinking about UI. Developers coming from jQuery, vanilla JavaScript, or server-rendered templates build UIs imperatively: they issue step-by-step commands to update the DOM when something changes. React asks you to abandon that and think declaratively instead: describe what the UI should look like for any given application state, and let React figure out how to transition the DOM to match.

attrs.alt: Imperative vs declarative thinking: imperative code manually queries the DOM and updates each element after each event; declarative React code describes the UI per state and React handles all transitions automatically
attrs.title: Imperative vs declarative mental model

content.text: Imperative: Describing Transitions

In imperative code, you are always thinking about transitions — the journey from one UI state to another. Every possible event requires you to write code that handles moving the UI from its current state to the next one. As the application grows, the number of possible transitions multiplies and they become harder to keep consistent.

The problem is not that any single update is hard to write — it is that you have to remember every update, every time the state changes. When you add a new feature that also responds to the "liked" state, you must find and update every handler that changes
liked
. Miss one and your UI falls out of sync with your data.
Declarative: Describing States

In declarative code, you think about states — the possible shapes your UI can be in — not the transitions between them. For each combination of state values, you describe exactly what the UI looks like. React runs your description, compares it to the last one, and updates only what changed.
Notice that adding the "Share" button required no changes to the event handler. The entire description of the UI lives in one place — the return value of the function. When
liked
changes, every part of the UI that depends on it is recalculated automatically.
UI as a Function of State
The core mental model of React can be expressed as a formula:
UI = f(state)
— the UI at any point in time is a pure function of the application's current state.

This formula has a powerful implication: to know what the UI looks like right now, you only need to know the current state. You do not need to trace the history of events that led to this state. You do not need to inspect the DOM. State is the single source of truth.
Every possible combination of
loading
error
, and
users
is handled by the return statement — including the empty state that is easy to forget when writing imperative code.
Thinking in Components

The declarative model pairs with the component model: large, complex UI is composed from small components each responsible for one piece of the state. When you look at a React application, you see a tree — not a sequence of DOM mutations, but a hierarchy of UI responsibilities.
The practical exercise for building this intuition is to take any UI mockup and break it into boxes before writing any code:
Draw boxes around distinct visual regions.
A product card, a search bar, a sidebar — each is a candidate component.
Name each box.
If you can describe what it does in one phrase (
FilterBar
ProductCard
CartSummary
), it should probably be a component.
Identify which component owns which data.
Data flows down via props. The component that needs to share data with siblings must lift that state to their closest common ancestor.
Arrange into a hierarchy.
Outer boxes contain inner boxes — this maps directly to the component tree in your code.
Worked example: a search page

Every UI state — loading, empty, results, initial — is captured in the return value. Adding a new state (e.g., an error) requires adding one more condition to the JSX, not hunting through event handlers.
Rendering as a Pure Calculation

React treats rendering as a calculation — like calling a mathematical function. Given the same inputs (props and state), the function must always return the same JSX output. No side effects, no DOM mutations, no global variable writes inside the render.
Allowed in render
Not allowed in render
Read props and state
Modify props or state directly
Compute derived values
Call APIs or fetch data
Return JSX
Read or write to localStorage
Call other pure functions
Subscribe to events or timers
Use array methods (map, filter)
Generate random values (Math.random)

This purity is what enables React's optimisation strategies: it can skip re-renders when props and state have not changed, run renders out-of-order, and safely discard partial renders. None of these would be safe with side effects in the render body.
State as the Single Source of Truth

The declarative model only works if there is one authoritative version of each piece of data. React enforces this through the component hierarchy: each piece of state lives in exactly one component. Other components that need it receive it as props.
Lifting state up

When two components need the same piece of data, the state belongs in their closest common ancestor. This is called lifting state up, and it is the primary tool for keeping a single source of truth in React applications.
Before lifting:
each sibling has its own copy of the data → they can diverge.
After lifting:
the parent holds one value and passes it as props → siblings always see the same data.
Thinking in Re-Renders

In the declarative model, every update is a re-render. When state changes, React calls your component function again with the new state and produces a fresh description of the UI. This is not expensive — React does not touch the DOM for parts that did not change — but understanding it helps you reason about your code.

The mental model: each render is an independent snapshot of the UI at a point in time. Event handlers and effects "close over" the state from the render they belong to. If you need to read the latest state inside an effect, use the functional update form of the setter.
Debugging with the Declarative Mental Model

The declarative model gives you a powerful debugging heuristic: if the UI looks wrong, the state is wrong. You no longer need to trace through a sequence of DOM mutations to understand what happened. Instead, you inspect the current state and ask: "If the state is X, does the component return the correct JSX?"
Open React DevTools and inspect the component's state.
If state is correct but UI is wrong: the render function has a bug.
If state is wrong: trace back to where the state setter is being called incorrectly.
If state changes but UI does not update: you may have mutated state in place instead of replacing it.
This debug flow is dramatically simpler than the imperative alternative, where you have to trace which event handler ran, in what order, and what DOM mutations it made.
Common Mistakes from the Old Mental Model

Developers with a jQuery or vanilla JS background typically make a consistent set of mistakes when they first use React. These all trace back to bringing the imperative mental model into a declarative context.
Imperative habit
React equivalent
document.querySelector
to read current UI
Read from state — state IS the UI
Mutating a variable after an event
Call the state setter, React re-renders
Showing/hiding via CSS display:none
Conditional rendering:
{show && <El />}
Appending DOM elements in a loop
Return
items.map(i => <Item key={i.id} />)
Reading DOM to determine state
Read state variable — never read back from DOM
The Two Rules to Internalise

Virtually all of React's design decisions follow from two foundational rules. Once you internalise these, patterns like lifting state, one-way data flow, and the need for keys in lists all make immediate sense:
State drives the UI.
When you want the UI to change, change the state. Never modify the DOM directly. If you find yourself writing
document.getElementById
inside a React component's render or event handler, stop — put that value in state instead.
The UI is a pure function of state.
Given the same state, a component always renders the same JSX. Side effects (API calls, timers, subscriptions) belong in effects (
useEffect
), not in the render body.
Practical Exercise: Rewrite Imperatively
A good exercise to cement the mental model: take a piece of imperative jQuery or vanilla JS UI code you have written before and ask: what is the state? Then rewrite it in React by:
Listing all the UI states the old code handled (loading, empty, error, success, each variant of each).
Representing each as a state variable in
useState
Writing one JSX return that describes the UI for every combination of those values.
Checking that you covered the same cases as the original — especially the ones that were easy to miss imperatively (e.g., the loading+error overlap, the empty list after a successful fetch).
Most developers report that the React version is noticeably shorter and covers more edge cases, precisely because describing UI states forces you to enumerate them.
When Declarative Thinking Gets Hard

The declarative model is not always obvious for interactions that are inherently procedural or that touch the outside world. Two areas cause friction for most newcomers: animations and third-party DOM libraries.
Animations

CSS transitions and CSS animations are fully declarative — you describe the start and end state, and the browser handles the motion. This works perfectly with React's model. Where it gets complicated is with JavaScript-driven animations (Framer Motion, GSAP, Web Animations API), because those are inherently time-sequenced.
Third-party DOM libraries
Libraries like chart renderers (Chart.js, D3 direct DOM mode) expect to own a DOM node and control it imperatively. The React pattern for this is to use a
ref
to obtain the real DOM node, hand it to the library in a
useEffect
, and then never touch that node with React JSX:
This is an "escape hatch" from the declarative model. React provides
useRef
and
useEffect
precisely for these situations. The principle: use the escape hatch for the narrowest slice of the UI that requires imperative control, and keep everything else declarative.
Modeling Complex UI States
One of the biggest wins from declarative thinking is that it forces you to enumerate your UI states explicitly. This surfaces edge cases that imperative code tends to miss.
Consider a form with three independent booleans:
isSubmitting
isSuccess
hasError

. That creates 8 possible combinations — but several are impossible (you cannot be both submitting and successful simultaneously). Modelling this as a discriminated union instead of three booleans makes illegal states unrepresentable:
This pattern — making illegal states unrepresentable — is one of the clearest expressions of the declarative mental model applied to state design.
Controlled vs Uncontrolled Components
HTML form inputs have their own internal state (what the user has typed). React offers two patterns for working with this:
Pattern
How it works
When to use
Controlled
React state drives the input value; every keystroke calls
onChange
which updates state
Validation, dependent fields, formatted input
Uncontrolled
DOM owns the value; React reads it via a ref on submit
Simple forms where you only need the final value

Controlled inputs are the "more React" choice because the input's value is always derived from state — the declarative model fully applies. Uncontrolled inputs are a pragmatic escape hatch for simple cases.
Summary
Imperative code describes transitions (how to get from A to B). Declarative code describes states (what A looks like, what B looks like).
React's core formula:
UI = f(state)
. State is the single source of truth.
When you want the UI to change, change the state — never touch the DOM directly.
Render functions must be pure: same inputs → same JSX output, no side effects.
Shared state is lifted to the nearest common ancestor to maintain a single source of truth.
To debug: check the state first. If state is correct, the render function has a bug. If state is wrong, trace the setter calls.
